findings: Banned child models are explicitly listed twice in /Users/greggcochran/dev/terminal-stampede/agents/stampede-commander.agent.md:106-111 and :203-207: claude-haiku-4.5, gpt-5.4-mini, gpt-5-mini, gpt-4.1.

The commander enforces the ban at the prompt/spec level by requiring every Squad Lead/Worker launch to set an explicit premium-model override from the approved pool (/Users/greggcochran/dev/terminal-stampede/agents/stampede-commander.agent.md:150-157) and by requiring launch_started telemetry to record the chosen model (/Users/greggcochran/dev/terminal-stampede/agents/stampede-commander.agent.md:166-168).

If a child launch falls back to a banned model, the documented outcome is not a hard stop; the commander must mark the bundle partial and report the violation (/Users/greggcochran/dev/terminal-stampede/agents/stampede-commander.agent.md:167-168).

depth-guard-lint.sh does not truly validate banned-model compliance. It only checks that the commander text contains model_policy=premium, Premium Model Policy, and gpt-5.5 (/Users/greggcochran/dev/terminal-stampede/tests/depth-guard-lint.sh:35-53), but it does not assert the banned-model list, detect banned names, or verify runtime enforcement.

Enforcement gap/bypass: the ban is policy-text only in these files. A commander/squad lead could still launch a banned model if runtime tooling ignores the prompt, because the lint test is string-based and there is no hard validation of child-agents.jsonl entries against banned_child_models in /Users/greggcochran/dev/terminal-stampede/tests/depth-guard-lint.sh:35-63.
